looking at moving more to that model in, in some way.
So we thought we would bring some stuff which obviously stands out for
us. Unitary authorities, as you know, do both regional and local
authority functions, both which will change substantially under this
new system. This means that the impacts of these bills for
Gisborne District Council and other unitary authorities are
different to those that regional and local councils will
experience. This is not recognized in the bills as
they are currently drafted. In fact, there are almost no
specific mention of unitary authorities at
all. A key example of how unitary authorities will be
affected differently to others is in the preparation
and also then the implementation of the new plans.
So let me use some examples with you.
Unitary authorities will have to, having already prepared the
regional spatial plan, prepare both a land
use plan and an environmental plan at the
same time, without any provision for additional
time to achieve this. Preparing three separate parts
of a combined plan will result in unnecessary
duplication and inefficiency. So
this duplication and inefficiency will then carry through to
how we implement these plans. The bills require
both a planning consent and a natural resources
permit, which in unitary regions will be issued by the same
council. For us, this is a backwards step,
as we currently only require one
consent under one plan. So what we are
asking is we seek explicit, explicit identification of
unitary authorities in this bill, and provisions allowing
us to avoid wasting time and resources, because that's ultimately
why the government is changing the bill, and we are happy to
discuss this, with officials in detail later, and it is addressed in
our, submission as well. Then I take you to our second point
is the implementation timeframes.
Um,
it is the timeframes for the development of the first generation plans
is not workable at all. We would be
given six months from the release of national
directions to prepare a full regional
spatial plan, which is not
doable when you look at the requirement for what this plan must
include, and the importance of the plan for the future prosperi-
prosperity of our region. In our submission, we have
suggested 15 months from the release of the national
direction would, would, would, would be more appropriate.
But I also know that some of our colleagues in other regions, such
as Canterbury, have s- said maybe what about 24
months to prepare robust first generation
spatial plans. Now the next, the nine-month timeframe for
preparing land use as well as na- natural environment
plans is unreasonable, particularly for unitary
authorities like us who have to prepare both.
This should be extended for unitary authorities to recognize our dual
workload to at least 18 months. Next, I would like to
quickly touch on regulat- regulatory relief.
Um, introducing regulat- relief in the current form,
will not work and it, it's aligned with rate
caps. The potential financial cost on re- of regulatory
relief will affect council's ability to keep funding within the
rates cap, while maintaining provision of
vital infrastructure and services.
This will be particularly felt in regions and districts like us here in
the T- Te Tairāwhiti, who has a very low rating
base. Transitional provisions resulting in historic claims
will exacerbate this and force our current community to pay
for previous private land use choices.
We can also see the regulatory relief resulting in a proliferation of
litigation through the Environment Court, which will just add to the cost
required to be borne by our ratepayers.
There's also a high probability that if regulatory relief is
not removed from the bills, protection of significant
sites will be unaffordable, and this will lead to further degradation
of biodiversity and natural, historical, and cultural
features across Te Tairāwhiti, and possibly the entire
country. Despite these concerns, we acknowledge that
commitments have been made for regulatory relief to be included in this
reform. Our submission is that if it is to
stay, sensible changes need to be made, and we've
made some, suggestions in our written
submission. And then second to last,
I would like to talk a little bit about sustainable land use
transition. Um, you
know what's been happening in the Te Tairāwhiti in the last few years,
what happens when we see these, extreme weather
events, and this is exacerbated by
unsustainable land use of highly erosion-prone
land, and how this relates to the proposals in this
bill. Our Sustainable Land Use Transition
program is the blueprint we will use toMove
our region forward. It has set out a bold but achievable
vision to transition highly erosion, erosion-prone land to permanent
resilient vegetative cover. We are investigate-
investigating s- certified sustainable land use plans as a
key transition delivery mechanism.

explicit that integration of freshwater farm
plans and property-specific erosion management

permitted. Council has applied, applied for a plan stop
exemption to progress our sustainable land use plan change to
address land with high risk of erosion as we await this
decision. It is vital that we can carry such provisions
over to the new system to be enable this
transition to carry on. Explicit provisions
must be made in both bills for regions like us with extreme
natural, hazard risk to apply more stringent
rules than national defaults, rather than leaving this to
be addressed in national instruments.
We have started on this transition journey, and we need to make sure that the new
planning system provides the regulatory levers
required to get that right. And then just a few final
points. Um, te tiriti partnerships with Mana Whenua on protecting
Maori rights and interests are very important to us here, and

carefully. And then secondly, the overlapping timeframes of

funding, rates capping, all-

Um, I'm Andrea Marshall. I'm the head of Environmental Planning and Sustainability
at Auckland Airport, and I'm joined by Lucy Edwards, Principal
Environmental Planner, and Clodah O'Connor McKenna, our Government
Affairs Manager. Overall, Auckland Airport supports the
intent of the bills and their aim to simplify planning to promote
growth. However, as set out in our
submission, there are certain changes to the bills that we consider would
further enhance the protection of significant
infrastructure. Um, Auckland Airport is New Zealand's
gateway. Um, more than two-thirds of New Zealand's international visitors
enter the country via Auckland, with twenty-seven airlines
flying to forty-two destinations around New Zealand and the world.
And planes don't just carry people, they carry high-value
freight. Um, Auckland Airport is New Zealand's third largest
port by value of goods moved. One daily
wide-body flight over a year brings in more than a hundred and
fifty million dollars of tourism spend and carries half a
billion dollars of freight.
All that economic value relies on infrastructure that's provided by
Auckland Airport. We are currently investing significantly
to increase resilience and to lift the capacity of the
airport. Because of this contribution to the
economy, we see it as critical that the planning system preserves
Auckland Airport's ability to operate twenty-four seven and to
grow as demand dictates. However, while at the
same time, we need to recognize that we exist within the wider
South Auckland community, and we're unable to internalize all
our noise effects.
I'll now hand over to Lucy, who will speak, about three specific
aspects of our submission.
Thank you. Um, I'll start with reverse sensitivity.
Auckland Airport recognizes the importance of enabling residential development and
supports growth occurring in the right places through a managed planning
framework that protects both communities and nationally significant
infrastructure. Aircraft noise is an unavoidable
consequence of operating an airport.
Where noise-sensitive activities, such as residential dwellings or schools, are
established or intensified in areas of high aircraft noise, this
can result in significant annoyance and adverse health effects on
residents. A World Health Organization study shows that at noise
levels within Auckland Airport's moderate aircraft noise area,
approximately thirty-six to forty-six percent of people are likely to be highly
annoyed. In the high aircraft noise area, this increases
to around forty-six to sixty percent.
At high levels, aircraft noise interrupts speech, both indoors and
outdoors, and disturbs sleep, with international studies documenting
associated health effects. At current
levels, there is approximately one arrival every four to five minutes on a peak
day between seven AM and ten PM, and this will inc-increase to around
every three to four minutes by twenty thirty-eight.
In a typical single flyover event at the outer edge of the moderate aircraft
noise area, it may be necessary for residents in the outdoor environment to
shout or pause a conversation for approximately thirty seconds every time an
aircraft passes, resulting in frequent interruptions to
conversation. In turn, this
gives rise to complaints against the airport's operations and submissions on
planning processes seeking to constrain those operations.
This is known as a reverse sensitivity effect.
Existing planning tools, such as aircraft noise overlays, play a critical
role in managing this risk. This is done in a balanced way,
allowing noise sensitive development in the right places, while ensuring that
the infrastructure that supports economic growth can continue to operate and
develop. Reverse sensitivity is not a
theoretical concern. Overseas, similar pressures have led to curfews and
movement caps at major airports, with serious consequences for
connectivity and economic performance.
The existing planning framework already includes a curfew that will place
operational constraints on Auckland Airport's future second
runway. Given that global aircraft slot management
dictates international flight schedules, Auckland Airport typically
can't choose when flights come in or depart.
Any further nighttime restrictions would be particularly damaging given Auckland
Airport's role as New Zealand's gateway and a critical freight
hub.
As set out in our submission, while standardization may be appropriate in certain
circumstances, it's essential that the new system retains flexibility
for councils to include bespoke provisions where operational
requirements or the management of the reverse sensitivity effects
necessitate a tailored approach.
This leads into my second point around regulatory relief.
Auckland Airport agrees that the proposed regulatory relief regime should not apply
to planning controls that are necessary for the safe and efficient operation of
infrastructure or the protection of public health and safety.
For example, aircraft noise overlays and obstacle limitation
surfaces. These controls protect health and safety and
operational certainty. They are localized, tested, and already
embedded in the planning framework and land use expectations.
If the regulatory relief regime is extended to these provisions,
there is a real risk that council will choose not to include them due to
cost. It is a crit-- it is critical that the scope of any regulatory
relief regime does not undermine the use of these carefully developed and tested
controls, and Auckland Airport does not have a view on that-- on the wider issue
of, of regulatory reliefOur
final point is that engagement with infrastructure providers is critical.
Auckland Airport supports the direction of the new bills.
However, they leave much of the detail to be decided through national instruments
and regional spatial planning.
It is vital that infrastructure providers are afforded adequate time to input
into these instruments so that we get durable, workable rules the
first time. If we rush this, we risk locking in the
wrong national settings and creating unintended consequences for
infrastructure providers and communities.
Further time and cost would have to be spent down the track revising the
documents, resulting in uncertainty and potentially delay
infrastructure delivery. We have therefore suggested amendments within
our submission that would ensure that there is sufficient time for
stakeholders, including infrastructure providers, to provide meaningful
feedback. Thank you for your time today, and we are happy to take
any questions.
Yeah, thank you. Thank you very much for your submission. Um, great.
Uh, well, I'll, I'll kick it off. The, y- I'm really intrigued
with your, um... So the regulatory relief, you've
obviously got exemptions currently under the, w- with
the, with what we've, from what we're proposing.
Is, is your reading of the proposed bills, is that carrying over
or not?
Yeah. So we are happy... But we sort of don't have a view on the wider regulatory
relief, but we just think that-
Yeah, I, I appreciate that, but yeah
... not extended to... We know that some submitters have sought that that regime is
extended. Um, we think that the controls we're talking about are fundamental
planning controls. They're not sort of optional planning rules.
Mm.
Um, they're localized, limited in geo-geographic consent, and
they're already embedded into land use expectations.
So, yeah, our submi- key submission point is that just that that regime
is not extended to those controls.
Understand. Understand. Okay, got that.
Um, Ryan Hamilton has a question.
Uh, thanks, Chair. Uh, obviously, most of your submission
points were fairly straightforward.
I just, o- obviously, a big key for you guys is ensuring those existing
designations roll over.
Mm.
Um, was there some concern when you were reading the draft
that they could be at risk?
Yeah. I think what we're seeking within our
submission is that I think to give effect to government's
intent to, to have more efficiency under the new system and reduce
litigation, there's the potential to ensure that
designations that are rolled over unmodified, are not
subject to re-litigation. Notice of r- of requirements currently
are, are comprehensive, costly, and, and very timely
processes. Um, and they often result in contested
hearings, and the, the, the final decision is sort of settled
planning outcomes. And currently as drafted, every time a plan
review happens, the, the whole designation is
open to, to that re-litigation, even if it's not being modified.
So what we've submitted is that, when designations are rolled over, that
public participation and scrutiny should certainly be available for those
modifications. Um, but when it's not being modified,
it should be rolled over as is to provide for, for certainty for infrastructure
providers.
Thanks. And a- another theme you've raised, which has been quite fre- frequent
also, is permitted activities not having to require registration.
It seems counterintuitive to the intent of the bill.
Yeah, completely. Um, and I know that's been, you know, as you say,
raised by a number of submissions, by s- a number of submitters.
We think that, yeah, if you required every permitted activity to
be registered, that's extremely onerous, not only for, the, the
developer or the plan user, but also for council.
Um, I think we operate under quite an enabling planning regime
currently. Um, we, we utilize a lot of permitted activities, and if we were
having to, um-
... notify council of each one, and then council had to register that and
then give us sort of a notification back that that permitted activity was
actually permitted-
Terrible
... that's, yeah, that's extremely onerous for us.
So, we're, we're keen that if, national
standards, expressly state that permitted activities can--
should be registered, then they have to be registered.
Uh, and national standards can, can also preclude those activities from being
registered. So, so that seems like a good way to get that balance.
Cool. Thank you. Thanks for that.
Um, I'm, I'm just... And a follow-on other question too, sort of similar.
I'm assuming that you have a number of designations for the, your,
operations?
Yeah. We currently have three designations for our operations.
Our key designation, the one that we sort of operate our
aircraft activities, and, and development
under, is sort of our, our biggest one, and then we also have
a designation for our obstacle limitation services, which is
sort of ensuring that activities surrounding the airport don't impinge on flight
paths and things, and then another smaller designation.
So yeah, we have, we have one key one that, sort of
enables our, our development and our aircraft
activities to operate, and then-
... another key one that protects those operations.
Yeah, I suppose, 'cause the point I'm sort of leading to there is we found with,
for example, Tauranga Port, they had different, con-
regimes, and they didn't all line up.
Yeah.
So one could come in at a different time and create issues.
Do you have anything like that? You know, that was, you know...
Do they all line up when they r- have to be re- redesignated or, or are they
separate timings?
Yeah. I think, and I can definitely see from a port's, you know, perspective in
terms of operating in the coastal environment and having to sort of obtain those
consents, that that's a real concern.
Um, Auckland Airport is really fortunate to have quite a
permissive and enabling pr- planning framework currently.
So we operate under those designations.
Um, under that sits a precinct with sort of enabling precinct provisions that
allow for sort of our, our activities and operations across the
precinct. Um, and so the key, the key thing for us is that
that enabling planning f- framework is enabled, under the new
system.
And just one final question. So just wanna understand something.
Like, with, obviously, with your designations and how your work,
like we struck a situation which has been more or less
resolved up in, in Whangarei where they put a, bought a,
um-- Whangarei Airport, they brought in the rescue helicopter services
came to be located. Even though it was an airport and there were operating
helicopters there, that led to a whole lot of d-
objection from, from local people.
Do you have any issues around that sort of thing? Where if you, if you, if you...
Or is your designation such that if you increase the number of flights
or within, within timeframes, do you have any problems in that
space?
So we are having problems with helicopter movements.
Um, so our-
Yeah. Yeah
... our designation is around the main flight, the flight
paths-
Yep
... associated with the runway. Um, when an introduction of a new
flight path for a helicopter pad,
within our designated land, that is presenting issues for us, and
yet it's, you know, a helicopter is core aeronautical activity.
Unfortunately.
And so that's bringing a new, regime because it's following,
a flight path that is outside of our traditional
contours.
And what... Yeah. Yeah. So, and so how, how are
you... So how are you handling that? 'Cause that's obviously...
I mean, I presume that could be a rescue helicopter or anything be based there in
that sense, couldn't it?
So we're having to treat that as a resource consent application
under, the unitary plan and
demonstrating, the number of flight movements
as sitting within the permitted activity, for the
noise standards for the wider region.
Um, what that has meant is it's really cur- curtailed the
extent that that helicopter pad can be used, even
though it's sitting on our land, and within our con- within our
designation.
Yeah. Okay. Yeah. No, we're just-- We've had similar issues up north, but
okay. Look, thank you. That's very good.
Um, no further questions it would appear, so look, thank you very much for your
submission. Very, very good. You've highlighted your key points well, and,
thanks a lot. Have a great day. Cheers.
Thank you.
Thank you.
See you. Bye. Righto, team, I believe that is
lunch. Okay. So we'll see you again twelve thirty
start.
